coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of silanized carbon dots with ficus virens leaves as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of iron ion
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Shuchen
Huang, Xiang
Lai, Lin
Sun, Wanlin
Chai, Shuiqin
Chen, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a green and economical strategy to prepare silanized carbon dots (Si-CDs) based on Ficus virens leaves was carried out. The prepared difunctional Si-CDs showed high water-solubility and favorable photostability. The morphology and surface functional groups were analyzed by TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. Si-CDs had amorphous nature with no crystal lattice and the average diameter of 1.95 nm. Some functional groups like nitrogen groups, oxygen groups, and silicon groups were discovered. Attributing to the small size of Si-CDs, positive charges, and the generation of singlet oxygen and hydrogen radicals, Si-CDs possessed effective antibacterial activity. The minimum inhibitory concentration of Si-CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus was 0.078 mg/mL. Moreover, Si-CDs exhibited selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching response to Fe3+ through static quenching process. The limit of detection was 0.84 μmol/L in the liner range of 5–50 μmol/L. Analysis of real samples led to 99.8–102.0% recoveries and <1% relative standard deviation. Consequently, this work discovered a new, green, economical silanized carbon dot with the potential application as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of iron ion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silanization
Antibacterial
Fluorescence sensor
Fe3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SEM images for bacteria after treating with Si-CDs
Selectivity of the Si-CDs fluorescent probe
The optimization of experimental conditions of Fe3+ detection
Relationship between fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs and the concentration of Fe3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microorganism
Chongqing University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Chongqing University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Scientific Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. KJQN201901501 ), the Open Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microorganism , Chongqing University of Science and Technology (No. GYFJWSW-06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, and b) TEM images of Si-CDs with different magnifications. (c) The size distribution of the Si-CDs particles. (d)FTIR spectrum of the Si-CDs. XPS (e), high-resolution C 1s (f), N 1s (g), O 1s (h), and Si 2p (i) spectrum of Si-CDs.
(a) Fluorescent excitation and emission spectrum of Si-CDs. (b) Emission spectrum of Si-CDs under different excitation wavelengths.
Growth inhibition zone of bacteria by carbon dots (a) Escherichia coli, (b) Staphylococcus aureus.

Antibacterial studies under visible light illumination against Escherichia coli (a) 0.039 mg/mL Si-CDs solution containing 0.1 g carbon source and 700 μL silicon source. (b) 0.156 mg/mL Si-CDs solution containing 0.5 g carbon source and 500 μL silicon source. (c) 5 mg/mL CDs solution containing 0.5 g carbon source. (d) Concentration-dependent antibacterial activity of Si-CDs.
The generated ROS of Si-CDs (a, b) singlet oxygen. (c, d) Hydrogen peroxide. (e) Superoxide. (f) Hydrogen radicals.
SEM image of bacteria treated by Si-CDs (a, b, and c) Escherichia coli. (d) Staphylococcus aureus.

(a)Fluorescence response to various metal cations. (b) The influence of concentration of Si-CDs to the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs. (c) The influence of concentration of Si-CDs to the fluorescence quenching efficiency. (d) The influence of different solvents to the fluorescence quenching efficiency.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of Si-CDs solution with the addition of Fe3+ from 0 to 100 μmol/L. (b) Linear plots of the fluorescence intensity of CDs against the concentration of Fe3+. (c, d) Fluorescence decay curves of Si-CD and Si-CD-Fe3+ systems, respectively. (e) Stern–Volmer curve of Si-CD-Fe3+ system. (f) Benesi–Hildebrand's curve of Si-CD-Fe3+ system. (g, h) The UV–visible spectrum of Si-CDs and Si-CDs-Fe3+ system.
Schematic diagram of the fluorescence response to Fe3+ for Si-CDs.
The minimum inhibitory concentration of carbon dots against Escherichia coli.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of silanized carbon dots with ficus virens leaves as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of iron ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microorganism, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microorganism
Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microorganism, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a green and economical strategy to prepare silanized carbon dots (Si-CDs) based on Ficus virens leaves was carried out. The prepared difunctional Si-CDs showed high water-solubility and favorable photostability. The morphology and surface functional groups were analyzed by TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. Si-CDs had amorphous nature with no crystal lattice and the average diameter of 1.95 nm. Some functional groups like nitrogen groups, oxygen groups, and silicon groups were discovered. Attributing to the small size of Si-CDs, positive charges, and the generation of singlet oxygen and hydrogen radicals, Si-CDs possessed effective antibacterial activity. The minimum inhibitory concentration of Si-CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus was 0.078 mg/mL. Moreover, Si-CDs exhibited selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching response to Fe3+ through static quenching process. The limit of detection was 0.84 μmol/L in the liner range of 5–50 μmol/L. Analysis of real samples led to 99.8–102.0% recoveries and <1% relative standard deviation. Consequently, this work discovered a new, green, economical silanized carbon dot with the potential application as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of iron ion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional spherical nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm [1]. The structure of CDs consists of a sp2/sp3 carbon core and a shell with some surface functional groups [2]. Now, green chemistry is a hot topic to develop an economical and green strategy. It was reported that nature carbon precursors [3–12] were prepared as versatile carbon dots through different methods. CDs have been emerging as one of nanomaterials for antibacterial agents, fluorescent sensing owing to the high water-solubility, stable fluorescence property, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility during past decades [13–18].

Pathogenic bacterial infections are a leading cause of growing mortality rate and premium healthcare costs around the world, which have evolved into a more challenging problem with the overuse and abuse of antiseptics and disinfectants in clinic, agriculture and animal husbandry [19]. It was reported that there were two antibacterial mechanisms of carbon dots, namely, cationic properties and reactive oxygen species (ROS) [20]. A good deal of positively charged carbon dots can be synthesized via cationic compounds rich in nitrogen-containing groups [21]. It works in the way of combination between positively charged carbon dots and negatively charged bacteria, which can break down the membrane integrity of bacteria and disintegrate the cytomembrane [22]. The ROS include superoxide (•O2−), hydroxyl radical (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and singlet oxygen (1O2) and excessive ROS cause oxidative damage to significant biological macromolecules such as lipids, proteins and deoxyribonucleic acid [23]. The photodynamic inactivation treatment is less possible to induce drug-resistance with main advantages of nonspecific oxidative damages to biomolecules [24–27].

CDs have been successfully applied to fluorescent probes due to high quantum yield, favorable photostability, and robust chemical inertness, particularly in toxic heavy metal elements such as iron ion, aluminum ion, cobalt ion [28]. Iron ion is one of the most abundant and essential transition metal ions in natural environment, as well as involved in a variety of physiological and pathological processes including cellular metabolism, oxygen transport and exchange, transportation of nerve signals [29]. The abnormal level of iron ion can induce severe diseases like anemia and heart failure [30]. Moreover, the level of iron ion is also a significant indicator for the evaluation of water quality [31]. Therefore, it is of great practical significance to prepare a selective and sensitive probe for the quantitative determination of iron ion. The fluorescent probes, used for the detection of metal ions in real samples, have the advantages of simple operation without sophisticated instruments. Carbon dots have been perfectly applied to fluorescent probes because of their low toxicity, environment-friendly and light-resistant bleaching compared with the traditional metal materials [32].

Ficus virens leaves as a precursor is cheaper, safer and easier to obtain compared with toxic organic molecules. Herein, we converted leaves of Ficus virens into fluorescent silanized carbon dots in an economical and green method-hydrothermal method for the first time. Moreover, not only the silanized carbon dots could be used to inhibit the growth of bacteria, but also could it be quenched by Fe3+. The morphology and surface functional groups of the silanized carbon dots, such as silicon groups, carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, were observed by TEM, FTIR and XPS. We also assessed the fluorescence stability of Si-CDs under the various pH values, continuous illumination and increasing concentration of sodium chloride by fluorescence spectrum. In addition, with the addition and augment of silicon sources, antibacterial activity of Si-CDs based on Ficus virens leaves and the sensitivity towards Fe3+ were excessively improved. The quantitative detection of Fe3+ in environmental water samples was also tested. This research provided a new way for multifunctional fluorescence carbon dots and a prospective application in clinic and environmental analysis.

All reagents used in this work were of analytical grade and used in accordance with the standards. Ficus virens leaves were picked in Chongqing university of science and technology. Triacetoxy(methyl)silane, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride(NBT), tri(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris), 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA), and coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (CCA) and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenylte-trazolium bromide (MTT) were provided from Aladdin Co., Ltd. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), Sodium chloride, yeast extract, agar powder, tryptone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Instruments can be found in the supporting information.

Si-CDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method [33]. The detailed operation steps were as follows. Ficus virens leaves were cut and smashed into powders. The mixture of leaves powders, triacetoxy(methyl)silane, and ultrapure water were put into a Teflon-lined pressure vessel of 25 mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The obtained brown product was centrifuged for 3 min at 3000 rpm, and the large particles of the crude product were removed by 0.22 μm micro-pore film filter. The ultimate solution was set into refrigerator at 4 °C. The concentration of Si-CDs was measured by rotary evaporator.

The fluorescent excitation and emission spectrum were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescent stability of the obtained optimal Si-CDs was evaluated under the condition of different pH values (3–11), consecutive illumination, and increasing concentration of sodium chloride (0–1.0 mol/L), respectively. 1 mL of Tri-HCl buffer solution (0.05 mol/L, pH = 7.4) containing 70 μg/mL Si-CDs was illuminated for a consecutive time. The fluorescence intensity of 1 mL of Tri-HCl buffer solution (0.05 mol/L, pH = 7.4) containing 70 μg/mL Si-CDs and diverse concentration of sodium chloride was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The influence of pH was investigated by adding various volume of HCl or NaOH solution. In all the optical experiments, the Si-CDs were excited at 300 nm and their fluorescence emission at 430 nm with the slit of 3 nm.

Frozen Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus solution preserved with 25% glycerin were activated in an incubator shaker (37 °C, 180 rpm) to get the bacterial suspension (OD595 = 0.6–0.8). 5 μL of frozen bacterial solution was added into 5 mL sterile Luria-Bertani culture solution and cultured at 37 °C, 180 rpm for several hours to obtain optimal bacteria in exponential growth period (OD595 = 0.6–0.8). The OD595 value of bacterial suspension was measured by microplate reader. The activated bacterial suspension was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C.

The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) assay referred to the previous report [34] and some corrections were made. Experimental details were present in the supporting information. Zone of inhibition tests was investigated by pour plate method [14]. 50 μL of activated bacterial suspension was added to a conical flask containing LB agar medium which had been melted and cooled to about 45 °C. The cooled LB agar medium was poured in the Petri dish. Sterilized Oxford cups were set on the cooled solid medium. 200 μL of samples was added to each Oxford cup. The diameters of the zone of inhibition were measured after incubation at 37 °C for 12 h.

Bacteriostatic cures of Si-CDs were measured by the means of doubling dilution method in 96-well plates [34]. The operation steps were as follows. 100 μL of diluted bacterial suspension with concentration of 107 CFU/mL was added into the 96-well plates and mixed with 100 μL of diluted Si-CDs solution. The 96-well plates were incubated at 37 °C separately in the dark and light condition. The OD595 values of treated bacterial samples were measured by microplate reader every 2 h. The growth trend of bacteria under different concentrations of Si-CDs solution was named as bacteriostatic curve.

The Zeta potential of diluted Si-CDs solution, the mixture of diluted Si-CDs solution and activated bacterial suspension were measured by Zeta potential analyzer. 1 mL of activated bacterial suspension and 1 mL of diluted Si-CDs solution were mixed well and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h in incubator shaker (180 rpm) and then the Zeta potential of incubated suspension was measured.

The ability of the Si-CDs to generate singlet oxygen, hydrogen peroxide, superoxide, and hydroxyl radicals was evaluated by fluorescence spectrophotometer and ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The way of detection of ROS referred to the previously reported method and some corrections were made [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM images for bacteria after treating with Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL of activated bacterial suspension and 1 mL of diluted Si-CDs solution were mixed well and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h in an incubator shaker (180 rpm). The bacterial suspension treated by Si-CDs solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The clear supernatant was discarded, and precipitate was washed twice with sterilized phosphate buffer saline (0.01 mol/L, pH = 7.2–7.4). The precipitate was redissolved and fixed with 5% glutaraldehyde for 12 h at 4 °C. The bacteria were soaked in dehydration with ethanol. They were dried by vacuum and sputter coated with gold. The samples were observed by field emission scanning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the Si-CDs fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the Si-CDs fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection was investigated by fluorescence spectrophotometer in presence of diverse metal ions (Fe3+, Tb3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Sm3+, K+, Cu2+, Co3+, Na+, Dy3+, Eu3+, Hg2+, Ag+) to observe the change of fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence tensity of a 1 mL of aqueous solution containing 1 mmol/L metal ions and diluted Si-CDs solution was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of experimental conditions of Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the concentration of the fluorescence probe towards Fe3+ detection was investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of diverse concentration of Si-CDs solution. The fluorescence intensity of assayed solution containing 50 μmol/L Fe3+ and diverse concentration of Si-CDs solution with the excitation slit of 5 nm and the emission slit of 3 nm.

The issue whether the change of Si-CDs fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe3+ was dependent on the type of solvents was examined by adding different solvents compared to the water. The fluorescence tensity of a 1 mL of assayed solution containing 50 μmol/L Fe3+ and three types of solvent (water, PBS buffer, Tris-HCl buffer) was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (λex = 300 nm, λem = 430 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between fluorescence quenching of Si-CDs and the concentration of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the Si-CDs fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection was investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of diluted Si-CDs solution containing diverse concentration of Fe3+ (0–0.1 mmol/L). The liner relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the concentration of Fe3+ was analyzed by origin software.

The Zeta potential of a 2 mL of assayed sample containing 0.14 mg/mL Si-CDs solution and 100 μmol/L Fe3+ standard solution was measured by Zeta potential analyzer. The ultraviolet absorption spectrum of a 1 mL of aqueous solution containing 0.07 mg/mL Si-CDs solution and diverse concentration of Fe3+ standard solution was analyzed by ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. The change of Fluorescence lifetime of Si-CDs after the addition of Fe3+ solution was measured to determine the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The change of fluorescence lifetime of 0.14 mg/mL Si-CDs solution in the presence of Fe3+ was measured by the FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quenching constant (Ksv) and quenching rate constant (Kq) was calculated by the Stern–Volmer's equation (F0/F = 1+Kqτ0CFe 3+ = 1+KsvCFe 3+, F0 and F represent the emission intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, τ0 is the lifetime of Si-CDs). The number of binding sites (n) and binding constant (K) were obtained according to Benesi–Hildebrand's equation (lg((F0–F)/F) = lgK + nlgCFe 3+).

The obtained brown Si-CDs solution was prepared by the hydrothermal method. The microstructural morphology of Si-CDs was revealed by transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 1 a, and b). The TEM images show the Si-CDs are nearly monodisperse spherical particles with an average diameter of 1.95 nm (Fig. 1c). Si-CDs have amorphous nature with no crystal lattice. It was reported that the size distribution of antibacterial carbon dots was mostly less than 5 nm and the smaller size of carbon dots was conducive to entering and exiting the cell and showed excellent antibacterial ability [2].

The FTIR spectrum was recorded to reveal the surface groups of Si-CDs. From Fig. 1d, the intense signal band at 3393 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the –OH group [36]. The weak signal band at 2971 cm-1 represents the C–H bond stretching vibration [37]. The signal band at 1595 cm-1 is attributed to N–H bending vibration [38]. These functional hydrophilic groups improve their water dispersity. The signal bands at 1382 cm-1 and 1272 cm-1 originate from the stretching vibration of C–O bond and C–N bond [39], respectively. The characteristic signal bands at 1122 cm-1, 907 cm-1, and 779 cm-1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of Si–O–Si bond [40], the stretching vibration of Si–O–H group, and Si–C tensile vibration, respectively. The XPS spectrum was further characterized to examine elemental compositions of the Si-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1e, the full XPS spectrum shows several predominant elements and their atomic proportion, indicating the Si-CDs consist of C, N, O, and Si elements with the element contents of C (29.07%), N (0.71%), O (44.09%) and Si (26.13%). Moreover, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 1f) reveals the presence of C–C/C–Si/CC bond (284.8 eV), C–N/C–O bond (285.82 eV), and CO bond (288.96 eV) [36]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 1g) manifests three peaks at 399.45 eV, 400.36 eV, and 400.72 eV assigned to N–Si bond, N–C bond, and C–N–C bond, respectively [38]. The XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 1h) located at 531.15, 532.65, and 533.40 eV are attributed to Si–O bond, C–O bond, and CO bond, respectively [38]. In addition, the Si–C bond (102 eV), Si–N bond (102.55 eV), and Si–O bond (103.15 eV) are observed from the Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 1i) [39]. These XPS measurement results of mentioned chemical bonds verify the Si-CDs are prepared successfully which are consistent with FT-IR.

The fluorescent spectrum of Si-CDs was measured to reveal the optical properties of Si-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the fluorescent excitation and emission spectrum of Si-CDs show the maximum excitation peak (300 nm). Fig. 2b reveals the phenomenon of excitation-dependent emission, which is similar to other carbon dots [41]. The fluorescent spectrum of CDs without silanization was also investigated to make a comparison with Si-CDs (Fig. S1). The maximum excitation peak had a slight shift from 330 nm to 300 nm with the silanization.

The fluorescence stability of Si-CDs was further estimated under continuous illumination (300 nm), various pH values, and different concentration of NaCl aqueous solution. Nearly, the fluctuation of fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs maintains a stable level under 1 h illumination (Fig. S2a). Fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs has no obvious changes with the increase of pH values (Fig. S2b). Fig. S2c presents that the increasing ionic strengths has a little effect on the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs.

The most efficient antibacterial silanized carbon dot was selected by adjusting the content of carbon source and silicon source in the way of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) assay (Table 1 ). As shown in Table 1, the increasing amount of carbon source has a negative effect on the antibacterial activity (sequence 1–5, and sequence 6–9). While the increasing amount of silicon source has a positive effect on the antibacterial activity (sequence 10–13). What's more, silanization can greatly augment the antibacterial activity compared with sequence 14 carbon dot without silicon source. Ultimately, the optimal silanized carbon dot, containing 0.1 g Ficus virens leaves and 700 μL Triacetoxy(methyl)silane, was selected as the experimental sample for further antibacterial tests for the consideration of high-efficiency and economy. In addition, the antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus was also tested, and its minimum inhibitory concentration was 0.078 mg/mL (Fig. S3).

The antibacterial activity of Si-CDs against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus was further studied using the agar-plate diffusion method (Fig. 3 ). Three kinds of carbon dots were selected to make a comparison, namely, sequence 2 carbon dot containing 0.1 g carbon source and 700 μL silicon source, sequence 3 carbon dot containing 0.5 g carbon source and 500 μL silicon source, sequence 4 carbon dot containing 0.5 g carbon source (sequence 1 represented water). Fig. 3 shows sequence 2 and sequence 3 carbon dots present obvious growth inhibition zone of bacteria. For the sequence 2 carbon dots, it has the most effective antibacterial activity and the diameter of inhibition zone to Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus is about 2.9 cm (Fig. 3a) and3 cm (Fig. 3b) respectively. What's more, the concentration-dependent inhibition was also studied (Fig. S4). There were fewer bacteria survived with the increase of concentration of Si-CDs. The antibacterial activity of Si-CDs was enhanced with the increase of concentration of Si-CDs.

To explore the antibacterial properties of Si-CDs, a series of photodynamic experiments were performed under visible light illumination with 16 W. As shown in Fig. 4 a, b, and c, growth curve of bacteria under visible light illumination underlies the dark one, indicting the presence of antibacterial photodynamic inactivation. The core carbon nanoparticles of carbon dots, associated with π-plasmons, have the broad-spectrum optical absorption of near-UV, visible light, and even near-IR [19]. Therefore, carbon dots can readily be activated by visible light or even under ambient room lighting conditions. The photoexcited carbon dots undergo rapid charge separation for the formation of electrons and holes, which are localized at passivated surface defect sites [42]. The generation of ROS is related to the charge separated species and the emissive excited states. It is interesting that the condition of visible light illumination has a positive effect on the bacterial growth when the incubation period is over 16 h (Fig. S5a). Moreover, the antibacterial activity of carbon dots in the dark was investigated using time-kill analysis (Fig. S5b, Fig. S5c, and Fig. S5d, and Fig. S6). Obviously, the concentration of carbon dots had a positive correlation with the bactericidal effect, which suggested the concentration-dependent bactericidal effect. As shown in Fig. 4d, the bacterial colony of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus on the LB agar plates are presented in the presence of different concentration of Si-CDs after 12 h of culture. 1 mg/mL of Si-CDs has significantly inhibitory effects on the Escherichia coli and kill almost all bacteria. In addition, Staphylococcus aureus could be completed inhibited at 2 mg/mL of Si-CDs.

Two common antibacterial mechanisms of carbon dots are the reactive oxygen species (ROS)-induced oxidative stress and cationic properties. This nonspecific oxidative damage can lead to the denaturation and inactivation of biomacromolecules like proteins, deoxyribonucleic acid, ribonucleic acid, and so on, which eventually causes the death of bacteria. In this work, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), Coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (CCA) were used to detect the generation of singlet oxygen, hydrogen peroxide, superoxide, and hydroxyl radicals respectively (Fig. 5 ). 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran is known as a singlet oxygen fluorescence quencher to detect the presence of singlet oxygen by forming non-fluorescent 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene. Fig. 5a shows the absorption peak of DPBF at 410 nm gradually decreases in the presence of the optimal Si-CDs (0.1 g carbon source and 700 μL silicon source) after illumination. There is no obvious change of DPBF absorption after illumination (Fig. 5b). HRP can catalyze hydrogen peroxide to oxidize 4-hydroxyphenylacetic acid (HPA) to form fluorescent dimers whose absorption peak is located at 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, no obvious fluorescent change at 450 nm is observed despite of the absorption of Si-CDs at 430 nm, which indicts the absence of hydrogen peroxide. NBT, a chemical probe that can form water-insoluble diformazan upon reaction with superoxide, was employed to determine the generation of superoxide. Three groups of experiments were performed to verify the presence of superoxide. However, all of experimental results suggested the absence of superoxide (Fig. 5e). The formation of fluorescent 7-hydroxy-coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (7OH-CCA) when CCA reacted with hydroxyl radicals was used to detect the generated hydroxyl radicals. As depicted in Fig. 5f, there is a red shift and intensified fluorescence intensity at 450 nm after reaction. In short, one of antibacterial mechanism of Si-CDs is the generation of singlet oxygen and hydroxyl radicals. The generated ROS of CDs without silanization was also investigated (Fig. S7), which suggested whether the ROS were generated was independent on the silanization, but the core sp2 carbon atom with π-plasmons.

The cationic properties of Si-CDs were further studied to reveal the antibacterial mechanism. Fig. S8 shows the zeta potential change of Si-CDs treated by Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus respectively owing to the combination between the positive charges of Si-CDs and negative bacteria. The external positive charges of Si-CDs are related to the presence of nitrogen element, which endows carbon dots with positively charged properties leading to the generation of ROS through electrostatic interaction with proteins, peptidoglycan, or phosphoric acid on the cell membrane [2].

The SEM images of bacteria treated by Si-CDs were visualized (Fig. 6 ). The cell membrane of Escherichia coli deformed and became loose after treated by Si-CDs (Fig. 6a, b, and c), attributing to the interaction between the positively charged Si-CDs and negatively charged membrane. Fig. 6d shows that the bacteria exhibit shrinkage and broke. The destruction of cell integrity is associated with the generated ROS of carbon dots.

When testing the fluorescent stability under various metal cations (Fig. 7 a), we occasionally founded that fluorescent Si-CDs had specific fluorescence quenching towards Fe3+, which suggested the potential application of fluorescence sensing. Fig. 7a shows the fluorescence of Si-CDs is selectively quenched. F0 and F represent the emission intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. Only treated by Fe3+, Si-CDs exhibited considerable fluorescence quenching response to Fe3+, which was related to a higher charge/radius ratio of Fe3+ compared with other mental cations, and nitrogen and oxygen groups could react with Fe3+ easier [43].

The comparison of fluorescence quenching response of Fe3+ between CDs and Si-CDs was also studied (Fig. S9). The ability of fluorescent quenching response to Fe3+ was significantly improved by silanization, which was related to the surface Si–O groups that can efficiently combine with Fe3+ compared with the simple carbon nanoparticle.

The influence of concentration of Si-CDs and different solvents to fluorescence sensing was investigated to optimize the experimental conditions of fluorescence response. As shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence intensity of Si-CDs with the excitation slit of 5 nm and the emission slit of 3 nm is enhanced in the range of 0.014–0.56 mg/mL, but decreases from 0.56 to 1.4 mg/mL due to the aggregation-caused quenching. Fig. 7c reveals the correlation of the concentration of Si-CDs with fluorescence quenching degree and the peak is located at 0.098 mg/mL of Si-CDs. Fig. 7d suggests different solvents have a dramatic effect on fluorescence quenching degree. The presence of phosphate buffer saline can counteract the fluorescence quenching due to the high affinity for Fe3+ of phosphate ions and the formation of Fe–O–P bonds between Fe3+ and phosphate-containing molecules [44], which can competitively combine with the binding sites of Si-CDs. Tris-HCl buffer has a buffering effect on the changes of pH value, which is conducive to the chemical structure of carbon dots.

For a sensitivity study, fluorescence changes were recorded with the gradual addition of Fe3+. Fig. 8 a shows the fluorescence spectrum of Si-CDs solution upon addition of Fe3+ ions at the concentration from 0 to 100 μmol/L. A gradual decrease of fluorescence change is observed, indicting the fluorescence sensing system is sensitive to the concentration of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 8b, the quenching efficiency has an extraordinary liner relationship with Fe3+ concentration in the range of 5–50 μmol/L with a correlation coefficient(R2) of 0.999. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.84 μmol/L was evaluated according to the equation-LOD equals 3σ/slope (σ is the standard deviation of blank sample, slope is the quenching efficiency/Fe3+ concentration ratio). The anti-interference capacity of Fe3+ sensor and the Fe3+ sensor in real sample were estimated to verify the feasibility of practical application (Fig. S10 and Table S1). The tap water was gained from the toilet of chemical engineering institute. As shown in Table S1, the recovery from tap water is 99.8–102.0% and the relative standard deviations for the sample is far below 5.0%, indicating the high precision and reliability of the method for the Fe3+ detection in water. Compared with other fluorescent nanomaterial probe to Fe3+(Table S2), Si-CDs had a low detection limit and it could be used as a potential fluorescent probe.

The potential application of green prepared Si-CDs in the antibacterial and detection of Fe3+ requires good biocompatibility. The cytotoxicity of Si-CDs to normal cells was investigated by MTT assays. As shown in Fig. 10 , the cell viability of incubated cells reaches 90% after 24 h in the presence of 100 μg/mL of Si-CDs, indicting the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of Si-CDs.

In summary, a now bifunctional silanized carbon dot with high water-solubility and favorable photostability were successfully prepared by one step hydrothermal method with the 4.6% of QY (Fig. S12) to inhibit the bacterial growth and detect the presence of Fe3+. For one thing, owing to the small size, surface positive charges, and the generated ROS of Si-CDs, Si-CDs have an excellent antibacterial capacity. ROS analysis shows Si-CDs can induce the generation of singlet oxygen and hydrogen radicals leading to the nonspecific oxidative damages. The experiments of cell membrane integrity reveal Si-CDs can break the membrane of bacteria. For another, Si-CDs have a high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ through the static quenching process between Si-CDs and Fe3+. The experiments of detection of Fe3+ in water samples by Si-CDs suggest Si-CDs exhibit satisfactory recovery, which confirm the good practicability. It is hoped that Si-CDs based on Ficus virens leaves will put into use as a potent antibacterial agent and an effective fluorescent sensor of Fe3+.
I have made substantial contributions to the conception or design of the work; or the acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data for the work; AND.
I have drafted the work or revised it critically for important intellectual content; AND.

I have approved the final version to be published; AND I agree to be accountable for all aspects of the work in ensuring that questions related to the accuracy or integrity of any part of the work are appropriately investigated and resolved.

All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript, including those who provided editing and writing assistance but who are not authors, are named in the Acknowledgments section of the manuscript and have given their written permission to be named. If the manuscript does not include Acknowledgments, it is because the authors have not received substantial contributions from nonauthors.

Conceptualization, S·P.; methodology, X.H.; software, X.H.; validation, X.H.; formal analysis, L.L.; investigation, L.L.; resources, W·S.; data curation, X.H.; writing—original draft preparation, X.H.; writing—review and editing, S·P.; visualization, S·C.; supervision, J.C.; project administration, S·C.; funding acquisition, P.S. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

This research was funded by the Scientific Technological Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. KJQN201901501), the Open Fund of Chongqing Key Laboratory of Industrial Fermentation Microorganism, Chongqing University of Science and Technology (No. GYFJWSW-06).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.
